== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
[[ja:笏谷石]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Stone materials]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Fukui City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20]]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
[[ja:笏谷石]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Stone materials]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Fukui City]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]]

Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}

revision.text:
[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

revision.text:
[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>

[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
[[ja:笏谷石]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Stone materials]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Fukui City]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[ja:笏谷石]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Stone materials]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Fukui City]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:Katsuyama stone]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Katsuyama stone-qq]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[Category:q--q]]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

revision.text:
[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>

Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.

Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.

Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>

== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: - '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. - '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. - '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': - Stone walls - Foundation stones - Bridge piers - Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': - Sarcophagi - Tombstones - Torii gates - Stone Buddhas - Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': - Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
