revision.text:
'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|12=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|12=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=draft}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|12=draft}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|12=draft}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|12=draft}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=draft}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|12=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|12=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=draft}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|12=draft}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|12=draft}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|12=draft}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=draft}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|12=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|12=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware

{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Stone materials]] [[Category:Fukui City]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] [[ja:笏谷石]] [[sn:Shakudani Stone]] [[sn:Katsuyama stone]] [[en:Katsuyama stone]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shakudani Stone}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Katsuyama stone}}

'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=draft}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|12=draft}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|12=draft}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|12=draft}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=draft}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|12=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|12=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture] <!--Katsuyama stone-->

After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

}}

revision.comment: 43 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:笏谷石 up to rev 98234200
revision.text:
'''笏谷石'''（しゃくだにいし）は、デイサイト軽石火山礫[[凝灰岩]]で、[[福井県]][[福井市]]の[[足羽山]]で採掘される[[石材]]である<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=ふくいミュージアムNo.60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=福井県立歴史博物館|page=4}}</ref>。狭義では足羽山<ref>近世”石谷山(しゃくだにやま)”ともいった{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm |title=福井県史通史編4近世二 |publisher=福井県 |date=1996 |accessdate=2020-10-21 }}</ref>北西山麓にあるいわゆる笏谷地区で採掘された石{{sfn|『石をめぐる歴史と文化』|p=9}}。一般的にはデイサイト軽石火山礫凝灰岩からなる笏谷層から採掘された石{{sfn|吉澤|2008|p=33}}のこと。
==成分== {{chem|SiO|2}}量は63～77%、{{chem|Al|2|O|3}}量は11～22%{{sfn|吉澤|2008|p=37 表1笏谷石の化学成分SQX分析結果}}。鉱物組成は石英、曹長石、灰長石、正長石、赤鉄鉱、方解石、緑泥岩等。
==成因と種類== 約1700万年前の火山活動によって噴出した火砕流に含まれていた、火山岩や灰などが堆積して凝縮した<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html 福井県みどりのデータバンク すぐれた自然データベース：足羽山の笏谷石（火山礫凝灰岩）]</ref>。堆積サイクルの境界には、「雲」と呼ばれる凝灰角礫岩や中粒凝灰岩などが入る{{sfn|笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る|1977|p=12-13}}。 石材は良質のものから順に、淡緑青色の青手、中手、灰紫色は黒手という。なかでも青手は越前青石もしくは青石といわれ、1.15倍の値が付いた<ref>敦賀運輸事務所 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>。
==特徴== * 青緑色で、水に濡れると深い青色に変化する。 * 柔らかく、加工に便利<ref name="#1">{{harvnb|笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る|1977|p=12}}</ref>。 * きめが細かい。 * 火に強い<ref>福井の文化財を考える会 1988, p. 11.</ref>。
==分布== [[福井市]]の[[足羽山]]の周辺に分布している{{sfn|吉澤|2008|p=38.図3「足羽三山の地質図」}}。
==人間史== [[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|笏谷石で造られた舟形石棺（丸岡城内で展示）]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|笏谷石採石場跡風景（七ツ尾口坑道より入坑）]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|笏谷石露天採掘場(不動寺境内)]] 1500年ほど前の[[古墳時代]]のものとされる[[石棺]]が発掘されている。[[朝倉氏]]の[[一乗谷]]遺跡では、笏谷石で彫られた石仏などが多数見つかっている。その後、[[朝倉氏]]を滅ぼした[[織田信長]]の家来である[[柴田勝家]]が[[越前国]]に入った際、[[北ノ庄城]]の屋根を瓦ではなく笏谷石の瓦で葺（ふ）いた。現在、[[柴田神社]]の境内である北ノ庄城址公園に、北の庄城の模型が展示されている。[[丸岡城]]も笏谷石の瓦で葺かれている<ref>坂井市教育委員会文化課丸岡城国宝化推進室 2019, p. 55.</ref>。半分が石造，半分が木造となっていた[[足羽川]]の[[九十九橋]]の、石造部分の橋脚にも利用されていた<ref name="#1"/>。 江戸時代には露天掘りと坑内掘りにより採掘され<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm |title=福井県史通史編4近世二 |publisher=福井県 |date=1996 |accessdate=2020-10-21 }}</ref>、[[北前船]]により全国に出荷されていた{{sfn|『石をめぐる歴史と文化』|p=38-40}}><ref>三井紀生『越前笏谷石』福井新聞社2002年、続編2006年、第3編2009年</ref>。[[1899年]]には笏谷石による[[継体天皇]]（継体大王）の[[彫刻|彫像]]を[[神体]]とした笏谷神社が創建された<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://info.pref.fukui.jp/keitaidaio/inside_place/fukui.html |title=県内の継体大王ゆかりの地 |publisher=福井県 |date=2012-02-15 |accessdate=2017-01-28 }}</ref>。1934年3月の調査では、採掘面積が約50町9段で<ref>敦賀運輸事務所 1934, p. 94.</ref>、採掘後は鉄道で輸送されていた。1945年舞鶴海軍工廠造機部の一部が福井第五五工場となり、笏谷石採掘坑道内に疎開した{{sfn|笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る|1977|p=15-16}}。
1998年9月<ref>{{Cite web|和書|author= |url=http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/06nisshi/2012/img/120208.pdf |title=緑の回廊 第4回「足羽川と足羽山シンポジウム」ポスター |publisher=特定非営利活動法人 エコプランふくい |accessdate=2018-07-15}}</ref>に採掘は終了し、現在は採掘されていない。2005年8月16日早朝、市営墓地の一部が東西約27メートル、南北約35メートル、深さ約18メートルのすり鉢状に陥没、崩壊する事故があり、地下の採掘坑跡の落盤{{sfn|吉澤|2008|p=36}}によるものである事が分かった。
==用途== *土木・建築用資材 **石垣 **礎石 **橋脚 **瓦(かつては、屋根の[[スレート|葺き石]]としても利用された。) *信仰に関するもの **石棺 **[[墓石]] **鳥居 **石仏 **狛犬 *日用品 **食器
==出典== {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite journal|和書|author=吉澤康暢 |year=2008 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf |format=PDF |journal=[http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/kenpou-5.html#55 福井市自然史博物館研究報告] |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |title=福井市足羽山の笏谷石と旧採掘坑道の陥没 |publisher=福井市自然史博物館 |accessdate=2023-10-16 |ref={{harvid|吉澤|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福井県立博物館 |title=石をめぐる歴史と文化 : 笏谷石とその周辺 |publisher=福井県立博物館 |year=1989 |id={{全国書誌番号|90012779}} |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000002018832 |ref={{harvid|『石をめぐる歴史と文化』}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=敦賀運輸事務所編|title=若越江に埋もれた次代乃物産を訪ねて|publisher=敦賀運輸事務所 |year=1934 |ref=harv}} * 「足羽県地理誌」、{{Cite book|和書|author=福井市(編)|title=福井市史、資料編10巻|publisher=福井市 |year=1991 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite journal|和書 |author=福井青年会議所指導力開発委員会 |year=1977 |title=笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る |publisher=福井青年会議所 |url=https://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000001-I050790115-00 |ref={{harvid|笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福井の文化財を考える会編|title=笏谷石文化|publisher=福井の文化財を考える会 |year=1988 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福井県立朝倉氏遺跡資料館編 |title=石の鬼 一乗谷の笏谷石 |publisher=福井県立朝倉氏遺跡資料館 |year=1983 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=坂井市教育委員会文化課丸岡城国宝化推進室編 |title=丸岡城 |publisher=坂井市教育委員会文化課丸岡城国宝化推進室 |year=2019 |ref=harv}}
==関連項目== * [[岩石]] - [[堆積岩]] - [[火山砕屑岩]] - [[凝灰岩]]
==外部リンク== * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html 福井県のすぐれた自然DB：足羽山の笏谷石（火山礫凝灰岩）]（福井県自然保護課） * [https://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/fukui/siryou/hen1/syou5/2kasen5.html 九頭竜川流域誌：第1編 自然と社会 第5章 川にまつわる文化 2.5 笏谷石]（国土交通省近畿地方整備局 福井工事事務所） * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ 笏谷石（しゃくだにいし）を訪ねて〜1500年の歴史と文化を再発見・ふくい〜]
{{Pref-stub|pref=福井県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しやくたにいし}} [[Category:石材]] [[Category:福井市]]

==用途== *土木・建築用資材 **石垣 **礎石 **橋脚 **瓦(かつては、屋根の[[スレート|葺き石]]としても利用された。) *信仰に関するもの **石棺 **[[墓石]] **鳥居 **石仏 **狛犬 *日用品 **食器
==出典== {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite journal|和書|author=吉澤康暢 |year=2008 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf |format=PDF |journal=[http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/kenpou-5.html#55 福井市自然史博物館研究報告] |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |title=福井市足羽山の笏谷石と旧採掘坑道の陥没 |publisher=福井市自然史博物館 |accessdate=2023-10-16 |ref={{harvid|吉澤|2008}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福井県立博物館 |title=石をめぐる歴史と文化 : 笏谷石とその周辺 |publisher=福井県立博物館 |year=1989 |id={{全国書誌番号|90012779}} |url=https://id.ndl.go.jp/bib/000002018832 |ref={{harvid|『石をめぐる歴史と文化』}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=敦賀運輸事務所編|title=若越江に埋もれた次代乃物産を訪ねて|publisher=敦賀運輸事務所 |year=1934 |ref=harv}} * 「足羽県地理誌」、{{Cite book|和書|author=福井市(編)|title=福井市史、資料編10巻|publisher=福井市 |year=1991 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite journal|和書 |author=福井青年会議所指導力開発委員会 |year=1977 |title=笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る |publisher=福井青年会議所 |url=https://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000001-I050790115-00 |ref={{harvid|笏谷石採掘跡の開発を探る|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福井の文化財を考える会編|title=笏谷石文化|publisher=福井の文化財を考える会 |year=1988 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=福井県立朝倉氏遺跡資料館編 |title=石の鬼 一乗谷の笏谷石 |publisher=福井県立朝倉氏遺跡資料館 |year=1983 |ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=坂井市教育委員会文化課丸岡城国宝化推進室編 |title=丸岡城 |publisher=坂井市教育委員会文化課丸岡城国宝化推進室 |year=2019 |ref=harv}}

{{Pref-stub|pref=福井県}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しやくたにいし}} [[Category:石材]] [[Category:福井市]]

'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|ja|福井県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in {{ill|Fukui City|ja|福井市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukui City|qq=}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|ja|一乗谷朝倉氏遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins|qq=}}. After the {{ill|Asakura Clan|ja|朝倉氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakura Clan|qq=}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|ja|柴田勝家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Katsuie|qq=}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|ja|丸岡城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruoka Castle|qq=}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|ja|足羽川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa River|qq=}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitamaebune|qq=}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[sn:Shakudani Stone]] [[ja:%E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] [[ja:笏谷石]] [[sn:Katsuyama stone]] [[en:Katsuyama stone]]
<!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--> [[Category:Building stone]] [[Category:Fukui (city)]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove invalid enwiki interwiki links
revision.text:
'''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[sn:Shakudani Stone]] [[sn:E7%AC%8F%E8%B0%B7%E7%9F%B3]] [[ja:笏谷石]] [[sn:Katsuyama stone]]
<!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--> [[Category:Building stone]] [[Category:Fukui (city)]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:Pages with no Wikidata]]

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}
