{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

{{wikidata link|Q11599510}}

More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|qq=|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|qq=|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

<!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--> [[Category:Building stone]] [[Category:Fukui (city)]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11599510}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}

Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has English label - based on Wikidata English label
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]

<!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--> [[Category:Building stone]] [[Category:Fukui (city)]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11599510}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--> [[Category:Building stone]] [[Category:Fukui (city)]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11599510}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]]{{wikidata link|Q1}}[[Category:qq]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}
[[ja:笏谷石]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]], [[:en:Fukui Prefecture|Fukui Prefecture]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in [[:en:Fukui (city)|Fukui City]].<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the [[:en:Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins]]. After the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura Clan]]'s fall, [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of [[:en:Maruoka Castle|Maruoka Castle]] also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the [[:en:Asuwa River|Asuwa River]].<ref name="#1" />
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the [[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]] trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| }}
[[ja:笏谷石]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:笏谷石]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11599510]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:26, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shakudani Stone''' (笏谷石) is a type of dacitic tuff composed of pumice and volcanic clasts, quarried from {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in {{ill|Fukui (city)|en|Fukui (city)|WD=Q201618|lt=Fukui City}}, {{ill|Fukui Prefecture|en|Fukui Prefecture|WD=Q133879|lt=Fukui Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.fukui.lg.jp/muse/Cul-Hist/info/kouhoushi/fm60.pdf|title=Fukui Museum No. 60|accessdate=2022-4-20|publisher=Fukui Prefectural Museum of History|page=4}}</ref> Strictly speaking, it refers to stone quarried in the Shakudani area on the northwestern slopes of Asuwa Mountain<ref>The area was historically called "Ishidaniyama" (石谷山). {{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4 (Early Modern Period)|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref>. More broadly, it includes stone extracted from the Shakudani Formation, a layer of dacitic pumice tuff.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=33}}</ref>
== Composition ==
Shakudani Stone is composed of {{chem|SiO|2}} (63–77%) and {{chem|Al|2|O|3}} (11–22%).<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=37}}</ref> Its mineral components include quartz, albite, anorthite, orthoclase, hematite, calcite, and chlorite.
== Formation and Types ==
The stone was formed approximately 17 million years ago from volcanic ash and rocks deposited by pyroclastic flows.<ref>[http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Excellent Natural Features Database: Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain (Volcanic Clast Tuff)]</ref> The boundary layers of its depositional cycles often include tuff breccia and medium-grained tuff, referred to as "clouds."<ref>{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12-13}}</ref>
Shakudani Stone is classified into three types based on quality: * '''Ao-te (Blue Stone)''': Light greenish-blue and highly valued. * '''Chu-te (Medium Stone)''': Intermediate quality. * '''Kuro-te (Black Stone)''': Grayish-purple stone.<ref>Dunshiro Transportation Office, 1934, p. 94-96.</ref>
Ao-te, also called {{ill|Echizen Aoishi|ja|越前青石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echizen Aoishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was considered the finest grade, valued at 1.15 times the price of other types.
== Characteristics ==
* Blue-green in color, darkening when wet. * Soft and easy to work with.<ref name="#1">{{harvid|Shakudani Quarry Development|1977|p=12}}</ref> * Fine-grained texture. * Fire-resistant.<ref>Fukui Cultural Heritage Society, 1988, p. 11.</ref>
== Distribution ==
Shakudani Stone is distributed around {{ill|Asuwa Mountain|ja|足羽山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuwa Mountain|1=Mount Asuwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637044}} in {{ill|Fukui (city)|en|Fukui (city)|WD=Q201618|lt=Fukui City}}.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38}}</ref>
== Historical Use ==
[[File:Ushigashima Sekikan.jpg|thumb|Boat-shaped sarcophagus made of Shakudani Stone (displayed in Maruoka Castle)]] [[File:Shakudaniishi quarry site trace.jpg|thumb|Quarry remnants of Shakudani Stone (viewed from the Nanatsuoguchi tunnel)]] [[File:笏谷石露天採掘場.jpg|thumb|Open-pit quarry of Shakudani Stone (on the grounds of Fudō Temple)]]
Artifacts such as stone sarcophagi from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} (circa 1,500 years ago) have been discovered. Many stone statues carved from Shakudani Stone were unearthed at the {{ill|Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|en|Ichijōdani Asakura Family Historic Ruins|WD=Q2968274|lt=Ichijōdani Asakura Clan Ruins}}. After the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura Clan}}'s fall, {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} used Shakudani Stone tiles to roof the {{ill|Kitanoshō Castle|ja|北ノ庄城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitanoshō Castle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 福井城|1=Fukui Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1073692}}. Today, a model of the castle can be seen in the grounds of the {{ill|Shibata Shrine|ja|柴田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shibata Shrine|1=Shibata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11534888}}. The roof of {{ill|Maruoka Castle|en|Maruoka Castle|WD=Q1143892|lt=Maruoka Castle}} also used Shakudani tiles.<ref>Sakai City Education Board, 2019, p. 55.</ref> Shakudani Stone was even used for the stone piers of the {{ill|Tsuzura Bridge|ja|九十九橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuzura Bridge|1=Tsukumo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370038}} over the {{ill|Asuwa River|en|Asuwa River|WD=Q3307289|lt=Asuwa River}}.<ref name="#1" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, Shakudani Stone was quarried using open-pit and tunnel methods.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T4/T4-3-01-02-05-07.htm|title=Fukui Prefecture History Vol. 4|publisher=Fukui Prefecture|date=1996|accessdate=2020-10-21}}</ref> It was distributed across Japan via the {{ill|Kitamaebune|en|Kitamaebune|WD=Q3440099|lt=Kitamaebune}} trading routes.<ref>{{harvid|Yoshizawa|2008|p=38-40}}</ref>
== Uses ==
* '''Construction Materials''': ** Stone walls ** Foundation stones ** Bridge piers ** Tiles (formerly used as slate for roofs) * '''Religious Artifacts''': ** Sarcophagi ** Tombstones ** Torii gates ** Stone Buddhas ** Komainu statues * '''Everyday Items''': ** Tableware
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Yoshizawa, Yasunobu |year=2008 |title=Geology of Shakudani Stone in Asuwa Mountain |journal=Fukui City Museum Research Bulletin |volume=55 |pages=33-46 |url=http://www.nature.museum.city.fukui.fukui.jp/shuppan/kenpou/55/55-33-46.pdf}} * {{Cite book|author=Fukui Prefectural Museum |title=History and Culture of Shakudani Stone |year=1989}} * {{Cite journal|author=Fukui Youth Council |year=1977 |title=Exploring the Development of Shakudani Quarry |publisher=Fukui Youth Council}} * {{Cite book|author=Sakai City Education Board |title=Maruoka Castle |year=2019}}
== External Links == * [http://www.erc.pref.fukui.jp/gbank/tokusei/d00226.html Natural Database of Shakudani Stone (Asuwa Mountain Tuff)] (Fukui Prefecture) * [http://www.fukui-shimin.jp/syaku/ Discovering Shakudani Stone: 1,500 Years of History and Culture]
{{translated page|ja|笏谷石|version=98234200|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:笏谷石]]
<!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--><!--Katsuyama stone--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11599510}} [[Category:Building stone]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fukui (city)]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
